name: Geometry
description: Plane and solid geometry — points, lines, angles, triangles, congruence, similarity, the Pythagorean theorem, circles, area, volume, coordinate geometry, and an introduction to formal proof.

modules.title: Points, Lines & Planes
modules.objectives: Identify and describe points, lines, planes, segments, and rays; apply postulates about points, lines, and planes; measure and add segments and angles.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.1: Geometry Basics (points, lines, planes, segments, rays)
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Basic Geometry Language and Notation
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Introduction to Geometry: Points, Lines, and Planes
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Basic Geometry Practice (15 problems)
modules.assignment: Draw and label 5 different geometric figures using proper notation. For each figure, list all the points, lines, rays, and segments. Identify which undefined terms (point, line, plane) apply and state two postulates that apply to each figure.

modules.title: Angle Relationships
modules.objectives: Classify angles by measure; identify complementary, supplementary, vertical, and linear pair angles; use the angle bisector theorem; solve problems using angle relationships.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.1: Angles and Their Measures
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Complementary and Supplementary Angles
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Angle Relationships and Angle Bisectors
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Complementary and Supplementary Angles Practice
modules.assignment: Find all missing angle measures in 5 diagrams involving vertical angles, linear pairs, and angle bisectors. Write an equation for each and solve. Classify each angle as acute, right, obtuse, or straight.

modules.title: Parallel Lines & Transversals
modules.objectives: Identify corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, and co-interior angle pairs; use angle relationships to find missing measures; prove lines are parallel.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.1: Parallel Lines and Transversals
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Parallel Lines and Transversals
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Parallel Lines Cut by a Transversal
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Parallel Lines and Transversals Practice
modules.assignment: Given 3 diagrams with parallel lines cut by transversals, identify all 8 angles, classify each angle pair, and find missing measures. Write a two-step proof showing that two given lines are parallel.

modules.title: Triangles: Classification & Properties
modules.objectives: Classify triangles by sides (scalene, isosceles, equilateral) and angles (acute, right, obtuse); apply the Triangle Sum Theorem; use the Exterior Angle Theorem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.1: Triangles and Their Properties
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Triangle Angle Sum Theorem
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Classifying Triangles and the Triangle Sum Theorem
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Angles of a Triangle Practice
modules.assignment: Classify 8 triangles by both sides and angles. For each, find all missing angle measures using the Triangle Sum Theorem. Apply the Exterior Angle Theorem to 5 problems.

modules.title: Congruent Triangles
modules.objectives: Identify congruent triangles using SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, and HL congruence postulates/theorems; write two-column proofs to prove triangle congruence.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Triangle Congruence Postulates (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, HL)
modules.assignment: For 8 pairs of triangles, state which congruence postulate (SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL) proves they are congruent, or explain why they cannot be proven congruent. Write a complete two-column proof for 2 of the pairs.

modules.title: Similar Triangles
modules.objectives: Identify similar triangles using AA, SSS~, and SAS~ similarity criteria; find missing side lengths using proportional sides and scale factors; apply similarity to real-world problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.3: Similar Triangles and Proportions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Similar Triangles and Proportions
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Similar Triangles: AA, SSS, SAS Similarity
modules.assignment: Determine whether 6 pairs of triangles are similar (state the criterion). For 4 pairs of similar triangles, find all missing side lengths. Set up and solve a real-world shadow or mirror problem using triangle similarity.

modules.title: The Pythagorean Theorem
modules.objectives: State and apply the Pythagorean Theorem; use the converse to classify triangles; solve problems involving 45-45-90 and 30-60-90 special right triangles.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.3: The Pythagorean Theorem
modules.assignment: Find the missing side in 8 right triangles using the Pythagorean Theorem. Use the converse to classify 3 triangles as acute, right, or obtuse. Solve 4 problems using 45-45-90 and 30-60-90 triangle relationships.

modules.title: Quadrilaterals & Polygons
modules.objectives: Classify and describe properties of parallelograms, rectangles, rhombuses, squares, and trapezoids; apply the polygon interior angle sum formula; find missing angles and side lengths.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.2: Quadrilaterals and Polygons
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Quadrilateral Properties and Theorems
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Quadrilaterals: Properties and Classification
modules.assignment: Complete a properties chart for parallelograms, rectangles, rhombuses, squares, and trapezoids. Find missing angle and side measures in 6 quadrilateral problems. Calculate the interior angle sum for polygons with 5, 6, 8, and 10 sides.

modules.title: Circles
modules.objectives: Identify and define parts of a circle (radius, diameter, chord, arc, central angle, inscribed angle); calculate circumference and arc length; apply the inscribed angle theorem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.2: Circle Properties and Circumference
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Central Angles and Arcs
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Circles: Arcs, Angles, and Arc Length
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Central and Inscribed Angles Practice
modules.assignment: Label a circle diagram with all parts. Find the circumference for 4 circles. Calculate arc length for 5 central angles. Apply the inscribed angle theorem to find 4 missing angle measures.

modules.title: Area
modules.objectives: Calculate area of triangles, parallelograms, rectangles, trapezoids, regular polygons, and circles; find area of composite figures by addition and subtraction.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.2: Area of Triangles, Quadrilaterals, and Circles
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Area of Circles and Other Polygons
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Area Formulas for Plane Figures
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Area of Triangles and Quadrilaterals Practice
modules.assignment: Calculate area for 2 triangles, 2 parallelograms, 2 trapezoids, and 2 circles. Find the area of 3 composite figures. Solve a real-world problem involving the area of a shaded region.

modules.title: Surface Area & Volume
modules.objectives: Calculate surface area and volume of prisms, pyramids, cylinders, cones, and spheres; apply formulas to solve real-world problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9.4: Volume and Surface Area
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Volume of Prisms, Cones, Cylinders, and Spheres
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Surface Area and Volume of 3D Solids
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Volume of Cones, Cylinders, and Spheres Practice
modules.assignment: Find the surface area and volume of 1 prism, 1 pyramid, 1 cylinder, 1 cone, and 1 sphere. Solve a real-world packaging problem determining which container holds more volume.

modules.title: Coordinate Geometry
modules.objectives: Apply the distance formula and midpoint formula; find slope and equations of lines in the coordinate plane; graph geometric figures and verify properties using coordinates.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9: Coordinate Geometry (distance, midpoint, slope)
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Distance Formula and Midpoint Formula
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Coordinate Geometry: Distance, Midpoint, and Slope
modules.assignment: Find the distance and midpoint between 6 pairs of points. Classify a quadrilateral as a rectangle by computing all side lengths and diagonal lengths using the distance formula. Write the equation of 3 lines given geometric information.

modules.title: Transformations
modules.objectives: Perform and describe translations, reflections, rotations, and dilations on the coordinate plane; identify lines of symmetry; compose two transformations.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9: Symmetry and Transformations
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Geometric Transformations
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Transformations Practice (translations, reflections, rotations)
modules.assignment: Plot a triangle on the coordinate plane and apply: (1) a translation, (2) a reflection over the x-axis, (3) a 90° rotation about the origin, (4) a dilation with scale factor 2. For a shape of your choice, identify all lines of symmetry.

modules.title: Introduction to Logic & Proof
modules.objectives: Write conditional statements; form the converse, inverse, and contrapositive; identify valid logical arguments; construct direct proofs using definitions, postulates, and theorems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Prealgebra 2e — Ch 9: Logical Reasoning and Geometric Proof
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Geometric Proofs
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Conditional Statements, Converses, and Two-Column Proofs
modules.assignment: Write the converse, inverse, and contrapositive for 5 conditional statements; determine whether each is true or false. Complete 2 two-column proofs involving angle relationships and parallel lines, supplying the missing statements and reasons.

questions.question: Which of the following is an undefined term in geometry?
questions.explanation: Point, line, and plane are the three undefined terms in Euclidean geometry — they are described but not formally defined.

questions.question: Two angles are supplementary. One angle measures 73°. What is the measure of the other angle?
questions.explanation: Supplementary angles sum to 180°. 180° − 73° = 107°.

questions.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. Angle 1 and Angle 2 are alternate interior angles, and Angle 1 = 65°. What is the measure of Angle 2?
questions.explanation: Alternate interior angles are congruent when lines are parallel, so Angle 2 = 65°.

questions.question: The angles of a triangle are in the ratio 2:3:5. What is the measure of the largest angle?
questions.explanation: The ratio parts sum to 10. Total = 180°, so each part = 18°. Largest = 5 × 18° = 90°.

questions.question: Which congruence postulate is illustrated when two sides and the included angle of one triangle are congruent to two sides and the included angle of another?
questions.explanation: Side-Angle-Side (SAS): two sides and the included (between them) angle determine a unique triangle.

questions.question: Two triangles are similar with a scale factor of 3:5. If a side of the smaller triangle is 12 cm, what is the corresponding side of the larger triangle?
questions.explanation: Scale factor 3:5 means larger/smaller = 5/3. Side = 12 × (5/3) = 20 cm.

questions.question: A right triangle has legs of 8 cm and 15 cm. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
In a 45-45-90 triangle, the legs each measure 7 cm. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
questions.explanation: In a 45-45-90 triangle, hypotenuse = leg × √2 = 7√2 cm.

questions.question: What is the sum of interior angles of a hexagon?
An inscribed angle in a circle intercepts an arc of 140°. What is the measure of the inscribed angle?
questions.explanation: Inscribed Angle Theorem: inscribed angle = ½ × intercepted arc = ½ × 140° = 70°.

questions.question: What is the contrapositive of the statement: "If it is raining, then the ground is wet"?
options: If it is not raining, then the ground is not wet.
If the ground is wet, then it is raining.
If the ground is not wet, then it is not raining.
If it is raining, then the ground is not wet.
questions.answer: If the ground is not wet, then it is not raining.
questions.explanation: The contrapositive negates and reverses the conditional: 'If not Q, then not P.'

questions.question: A line segment has endpoints A(2, 3) and B(8, 11). What is the length of AB?
Vertical angles are always:
questions.explanation: Vertical angles are the opposite angles formed when two lines intersect; they are always congruent.

questions.question: A 5-meter ladder leans against a wall, with its base 3 meters from the wall. How high up the wall does the ladder reach? Show your work using the Pythagorean Theorem.
questions.explanation: h² + 3² = 5². h² = 25 − 9 = 16. h = 4 meters.

questions.question: Two angles form a linear pair. One angle is expressed as (3x + 20)° and the other as (x + 40)°. Find the value of x and the measure of each angle.
Which of the following best describes a ray?
options: A set of all points in a plane equidistant from a center
A part of a line with two endpoints
A part of a line with one endpoint extending infinitely in one direction
Two rays sharing a common endpoint
questions.answer: A part of a line with one endpoint extending infinitely in one direction
questions.explanation: A ray starts at one endpoint and extends infinitely in one direction only.

questions.question: Two angles are complementary. One angle is (2x + 10)°. The other is (3x − 5)°. What is the value of x?
questions.explanation: Complementary angles sum to 90°: (2x+10)+(3x−5)=90 → 5x+5=90 → 5x=85 → x=17.

questions.question: Co-interior (same-side interior) angles formed by parallel lines cut by a transversal are:
questions.explanation: Co-interior (consecutive interior) angles are on the same side of the transversal and sum to 180° when lines are parallel.

questions.question: A triangle has an exterior angle of 115°. The two non-adjacent interior angles are (5x)° and (4x + 7)°. What is x?
questions.explanation: Exterior Angle Theorem: exterior angle = sum of remote interior angles. 5x + 4x + 7 = 115 → 9x = 108 → x = 12.

questions.question: Which congruence criterion works only for right triangles?
questions.explanation: Hypotenuse-Leg (HL) congruence applies only to right triangles: if the hypotenuse and one leg are congruent, the triangles are congruent.

questions.question: In similar triangles △ABC ~ △DEF, AB = 6, DE = 9, and AC = 8. What is DF?
questions.explanation: Scale factor = DE/AB = 9/6 = 3/2. DF = AC × (3/2) = 8 × 1.5 = 12.

questions.question: In a 30-60-90 triangle, the shorter leg is 5. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
questions.explanation: In a 30-60-90 triangle, hypotenuse = 2 × shorter leg = 2 × 5 = 10.

questions.question: A rectangle has opposite sides equal and all four right angles. Which property is unique to a rectangle (but not all parallelograms)?
questions.explanation: In a rectangle, the diagonals are congruent. This is not true of all parallelograms (e.g., a non-rectangular parallelogram has unequal diagonals).

questions.question: A circle has a radius of 6 cm. What is the length of an arc intercepted by a central angle of 120°?
What is the area of a trapezoid with bases 7 cm and 13 cm and a height of 5 cm?
A cylinder has radius 4 cm and height 10 cm. What is its volume?
What is the midpoint of the segment with endpoints (−4, 6) and (10, −2)?
A triangle with vertices A(0, 0), B(4, 0), and C(4, 3) is reflected over the y-axis. What are the coordinates of the image of B?
questions.explanation: Reflection over the y-axis maps (x, y) → (−x, y). B(4, 0) → B'(−4, 0).

questions.question: Which statement is the converse of: "If a polygon is a square, then it has four right angles"?
options: If a polygon does not have four right angles, then it is not a square.
If a polygon has four right angles, then it is a square.
If a polygon is not a square, then it does not have four right angles.
A polygon is a square if and only if it has four right angles.
questions.answer: If a polygon has four right angles, then it is a square.
questions.explanation: The converse switches the hypothesis and conclusion: 'If Q, then P.' Note that the converse is not necessarily true.

questions.question: A dilation with center at the origin and scale factor 3 maps point P(2, −4) to P'. What are the coordinates of P'?
questions.explanation: Dilation by scale factor k maps (x, y) → (kx, ky). P(2, −4) → (3×2, 3×(−4)) = (6, −12).

questions.question: What is the area of a circle with diameter 14 cm?
questions.explanation: Radius = 7 cm. A = πr² = π(7²) = 49π cm².

questions.question: A sphere has a radius of 3 cm. What is its volume?

A coordinate proof: Points A(0, 0), B(6, 0), C(6, 4), and D(0, 4) form a quadrilateral. Use the distance formula to show that ABCD is a rectangle (all sides meet at right angles, opposite sides equal).
questions.answer: AB = 6, BC = 4, CD = 6, DA = 4; all adjacent sides are perpendicular (one is horizontal, one is vertical), confirming a rectangle.
questions.explanation: AB: √((6−0)²+(0−0)²)=6; BC: √((6−6)²+(4−0)²)=4; CD: √((0−6)²+(4−4)²)=6; DA: √((0−0)²+(0−4)²)=4. Opposite sides equal, adjacent sides perpendicular (slopes 0 and undefined) → rectangle.

questions.question: A conical paper cup has a radius of 4 cm and a height of 9 cm. Find its volume. Then determine how many such cups of water are needed to fill a cylindrical tank with radius 4 cm and height 9 cm.
questions.answer: Cone volume = 48π cm³; Cylinder volume = 144π cm³; 3 cones needed.

questions.question: Triangle ABC has vertices A(1, 2), B(5, 2), and C(3, 6). Find the perimeter of triangle ABC. Round to the nearest tenth if needed.

quizQuestions.question: Which undefined term has no size, shape, or dimension and is represented by a dot?
quizQuestions.explanation: A point has no length, width, or depth — it simply marks a location in space.

quizQuestions.question: A segment is part of a line that:
options: Has one endpoint and extends infinitely
quizQuestions.explanation: A line segment is a portion of a line bounded by two endpoints.

quizQuestions.question: If M is the midpoint of segment AB and AM = 7 cm, what is AB?
quizQuestions.explanation: M is the midpoint, so AM = MB = 7 cm. AB = 7 + 7 = 14 cm.

quizQuestions.question: Through any two points, there exists exactly:
quizQuestions.explanation: One of the basic postulates of geometry: any two distinct points determine exactly one line.

quizQuestions.question: An angle is formed by:
options: Two rays sharing a common endpoint
quizQuestions.answer: Two rays sharing a common endpoint
quizQuestions.explanation: An angle consists of two rays (sides) that share a common endpoint called the vertex.

quizQuestions.question: Two angles whose measures sum to 90° are called:
quizQuestions.explanation: Complementary angles sum to exactly 90°.

quizQuestions.question: A linear pair of angles always sums to:
quizQuestions.explanation: A linear pair consists of two adjacent angles that together form a straight line (180°).

quizQuestions.question: Vertical angles are formed when two lines intersect. They are always:
quizQuestions.explanation: Vertical angles are the non-adjacent angles formed at an intersection; they are always equal in measure.

quizQuestions.question: An angle bisector divides an angle into:
quizQuestions.explanation: By definition, an angle bisector splits an angle into two equal (congruent) halves.

quizQuestions.question: If ∠A = (4x + 10)° and ∠B = (6x − 20)° are vertical angles, what is x?
quizQuestions.explanation: Vertical angles are congruent: 4x + 10 = 6x − 20 → 30 = 2x → x = 15.

quizQuestions.question: When parallel lines are cut by a transversal, corresponding angles are:
quizQuestions.explanation: Corresponding angles occupy the same position at each intersection and are congruent when lines are parallel.

quizQuestions.question: Alternate exterior angles are located:
options: On the same side of the transversal, between the parallel lines
On opposite sides of the transversal, between the parallel lines
On opposite sides of the transversal, outside the parallel lines
On the same side, outside the parallel lines
quizQuestions.answer: On opposite sides of the transversal, outside the parallel lines
quizQuestions.explanation: Alternate exterior angles are on opposite sides of the transversal and outside (exterior to) the two parallel lines.

quizQuestions.question: Two lines cut by a transversal have co-interior angles of 75° and 115°. Are the lines parallel?
options: Yes, because co-interior angles are supplementary
No, because co-interior angles must equal 180°
Yes, because co-interior angles are congruent
quizQuestions.explanation: Co-interior angles must sum to 180° for the lines to be parallel. 75° + 115° = 190° ≠ 180°, so the lines are not parallel.

quizQuestions.question: If alternate interior angles are congruent, the lines cut by the transversal are:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Converse of the Alternate Interior Angles Theorem states that if alternate interior angles are congruent, the lines are parallel.

quizQuestions.question: A transversal crosses two parallel lines. One co-interior angle is 58°. What is the other co-interior angle?
quizQuestions.explanation: Co-interior angles are supplementary: 180° − 58° = 122°.

quizQuestions.question: The Triangle Sum Theorem states that the interior angles of a triangle sum to:
quizQuestions.explanation: The three interior angles of any triangle always add up to exactly 180°.

quizQuestions.question: A triangle with all three sides equal is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: An equilateral triangle has all three sides congruent and all three angles equal to 60°.

quizQuestions.question: In triangle ABC, ∠A = 45° and ∠B = 75°. What is ∠C?
An exterior angle of a triangle equals the sum of the two:
options: Interior angles on the same side
quizQuestions.explanation: The Exterior Angle Theorem: an exterior angle equals the sum of the two non-adjacent (remote) interior angles.

quizQuestions.question: A triangle with one angle greater than 90° is classified as:
quizQuestions.explanation: An obtuse triangle has one interior angle measuring greater than 90°.

quizQuestions.question: The SSS congruence postulate requires:
options: Two sides and the included angle to be congruent
All three sides to be congruent
Two angles and a side to be congruent
The hypotenuse and one leg to be congruent
quizQuestions.answer: All three sides to be congruent
quizQuestions.explanation: Side-Side-Side (SSS): if all three sides of one triangle are congruent to the corresponding sides of another, the triangles are congruent.

quizQuestions.question: In a two-column proof, what goes in the right column?
quizQuestions.explanation: The left column contains statements and the right column contains the justifications (reasons) for each statement.

quizQuestions.question: AAS congruence uses:
options: Two angles and the included side
Two angles and a non-included side
quizQuestions.answer: Two angles and a non-included side
quizQuestions.explanation: Angle-Angle-Side (AAS): two angles and a non-included (not between them) side prove congruence.

quizQuestions.question: The HL congruence theorem applies only to:
quizQuestions.explanation: Hypotenuse-Leg (HL) applies only to right triangles, using the hypotenuse and one leg.

quizQuestions.question: If △ABC ≅ △DEF, which pair of angles must be congruent?
quizQuestions.explanation: Corresponding parts of congruent triangles are congruent (CPCTC). A corresponds to D in △ABC ≅ △DEF.

quizQuestions.question: The AA similarity criterion states two triangles are similar if:
options: Two angles of one equal two angles of the other
Two sides and the included angle are proportional
All three angles are equal in both triangles
quizQuestions.answer: Two angles of one equal two angles of the other
quizQuestions.explanation: Angle-Angle (AA): if two pairs of corresponding angles are congruent, the triangles are similar.

quizQuestions.question: Similar triangles have:
quizQuestions.explanation: Similar triangles have the same shape — corresponding angles are congruent and corresponding sides are proportional.

quizQuestions.question: The scale factor of △ABC to △DEF is 2:3. If AB = 8, what is DE?
quizQuestions.explanation: Scale factor AB:DE = 2:3. DE = AB × (3/2) = 8 × 1.5 = 12.

quizQuestions.question: A tree casts a 15 m shadow. Nearby, a 2 m post casts a 3 m shadow. How tall is the tree?
quizQuestions.explanation: Ratios of corresponding parts of similar triangles: 2/3 = h/15 → h = 10 m.

quizQuestions.question: If two triangles are similar with a scale factor of 1:4, how do their areas compare?
quizQuestions.explanation: Area ratio = (scale factor)² = 1²:4² = 1:16.

quizQuestions.question: In a right triangle with legs a = 6 and b = 8, what is the hypotenuse?
The converse of the Pythagorean Theorem states that if a² + b² = c², then the triangle is:
quizQuestions.explanation: If the square of the longest side equals the sum of squares of the other two sides, the triangle is a right triangle.

quizQuestions.question: A triangle has sides of length 5, 12, and 13. Is it a right triangle?
options: Yes, because all sides are integers
quizQuestions.explanation: 25 + 144 = 169 = 13². The converse of the Pythagorean Theorem confirms this is a right triangle.

quizQuestions.question: In a 45-45-90 triangle, if the hypotenuse is 10, what is the leg length?
In a 30-60-90 triangle, the longer leg is 6√3. What is the shorter leg?
quizQuestions.explanation: Longer leg = shorter leg × √3. 6√3 = shorter leg × √3. Shorter leg = 6.

quizQuestions.question: The sum of interior angles of a quadrilateral is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Interior angle sum = (4 − 2) × 180° = 2 × 180° = 360°.

quizQuestions.question: A parallelogram has one angle of 70°. What are the measures of the remaining three angles?
quizQuestions.explanation: Consecutive angles in a parallelogram are supplementary. The four angles are 70°, 110°, 70°, 110°.

quizQuestions.question: Which quadrilateral has exactly one pair of parallel sides?
quizQuestions.explanation: A trapezoid has exactly one pair of parallel sides (the bases). Parallelograms have two pairs.

quizQuestions.question: The formula for the sum of interior angles of an n-gon is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Any polygon can be divided into (n − 2) triangles, each contributing 180°.

quizQuestions.question: Which property belongs to all rhombuses?
options: Only one pair of parallel sides
quizQuestions.explanation: By definition, a rhombus has four congruent sides. It does not necessarily have right angles.

quizQuestions.question: The distance from the center of a circle to any point on the circle is called the:
quizQuestions.explanation: The radius is the fixed distance from the center to any point on the circle.

quizQuestions.question: A central angle of 90° in a circle with radius 5 cm subtends an arc. What fraction of the circle's circumference is this arc?
quizQuestions.explanation: 90° is 90/360 = 1/4 of the full circle, so the arc is 1/4 of the circumference.

quizQuestions.question: An inscribed angle that intercepts a semicircle (arc of 180°) measures:
quizQuestions.explanation: Inscribed angle = ½ × intercepted arc = ½ × 180° = 90°. This is Thales' Theorem.

quizQuestions.question: A chord is:
options: A line tangent to the circle
A segment from the center to the circle
A segment with both endpoints on the circle
The longest arc of a circle
quizQuestions.answer: A segment with both endpoints on the circle
quizQuestions.explanation: A chord is a line segment whose two endpoints both lie on the circle. The diameter is the longest chord.

quizQuestions.question: What is the circumference of a circle with diameter 10 cm?
quizQuestions.explanation: C = πd = π × 10 = 10π cm.

quizQuestions.question: What is the area of a triangle with base 12 cm and height 7 cm?
quizQuestions.explanation: A = ½bh = ½ × 12 × 7 = 42 cm².

quizQuestions.question: What is the area of a parallelogram with base 9 m and height 5 m?
A square has area 64 cm². What is the length of its side?
quizQuestions.explanation: A = s². s = √64 = 8 cm.

quizQuestions.question: A composite figure consists of a rectangle (5 × 4) with a semicircle (radius 2) on top. What is the area of the composite figure?
quizQuestions.explanation: Rectangle area = 5 × 4 = 20. Semicircle area = ½π(2²) = 2π. Total = 20 + 2π.

quizQuestions.question: Which formula gives the area of a regular polygon with perimeter P and apothem a?
quizQuestions.explanation: Area of a regular polygon = ½ × perimeter × apothem = ½Pa.

quizQuestions.question: What is the volume of a rectangular prism with length 4, width 3, and height 5?
quizQuestions.explanation: V = l × w × h = 4 × 3 × 5 = 60 cubic units.

quizQuestions.question: The volume of a pyramid is what fraction of the volume of a prism with the same base and height?
quizQuestions.explanation: V_pyramid = ⅓ × Base Area × height. It is exactly one-third of the corresponding prism's volume.

quizQuestions.question: What is the total surface area of a cube with side length 4 cm?
quizQuestions.explanation: A cube has 6 faces, each with area s². SA = 6 × (4)² = 6 × 16 = 96 cm².

quizQuestions.question: A cone has radius 3 and height 4. What is its volume?
quizQuestions.explanation: V = ⅓πr²h = ⅓π(9)(4) = 12π.

quizQuestions.question: What is the volume of a sphere with radius 6 cm?
What is the distance between points (1, 4) and (5, 7)?
What is the midpoint of the segment from (2, 8) to (6, 2)?
What is the slope of the line through (0, 3) and (4, 11)?
What is the equation of a line with slope 3 that passes through the origin?
quizQuestions.explanation: The line passes through (0, 0), so y-intercept b = 0. Equation: y = 3x.

quizQuestions.question: Two points on a circle are A(0, 0) and B(6, 0). What is the length of chord AB?
A translation moves a point (3, −2) by the vector ⟨−4, 5⟩. Where does the point land?
Reflecting the point (3, −5) over the x-axis gives:
quizQuestions.explanation: Reflection over x-axis: (x, y) → (x, −y). (3, −5) → (3, 5).

quizQuestions.question: Rotating the point (2, 3) by 90° counterclockwise about the origin gives:
quizQuestions.explanation: 90° CCW rotation rule: (x, y) → (−y, x). (2, 3) → (−3, 2).

quizQuestions.question: A dilation with scale factor k > 1 produces an image that is:
options: The same size as the original
quizQuestions.explanation: When k > 1, the image is enlarged. When 0 < k < 1, the image is reduced.

quizQuestions.question: Which transformation preserves both size and shape (is an isometry)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Rotations are isometries — they preserve distances and angle measures. Dilations change size, so they are not isometries.

quizQuestions.question: The hypothesis of the conditional statement 'If a shape is a square, then it has four equal sides' is:
options: It does not have four equal sides
quizQuestions.explanation: In a conditional 'If P, then Q,' P is the hypothesis (the 'if' part).

quizQuestions.question: The inverse of 'If P, then Q' is:
options: If not P, then not Q
If not Q, then not P
quizQuestions.answer: If not P, then not Q
quizQuestions.explanation: The inverse negates both the hypothesis and conclusion of the original conditional.

quizQuestions.question: Which form of a conditional is always logically equivalent to the original?
quizQuestions.explanation: The contrapositive ('If not Q, then not P') is always logically equivalent to the original conditional.

quizQuestions.question: In a two-column proof, the first statement is typically:
options: The conclusion you want to prove
A theorem from a previous chapter
quizQuestions.explanation: A proof begins with what is given (known) and uses logical steps to reach the conclusion.

quizQuestions.question: Which is a valid reason to use in a geometric proof?
options: It looks like it from the diagram
quizQuestions.explanation: Valid reasons in geometric proofs are definitions, postulates, previously proven theorems, and given information.
